questions.prompt: Which of these belongs to "what the runbook owns" rather than "what you own" during a bulk rollout?

choices.label: Watching the portal for new enrolments while the RMM job runs.
choices.rationale: That's the monitoring step; you own the running, which includes watching the portal.

choices: Which RMM group, OU, or device list the installer targets.

choices.label: Comparing the RMM success count to the portal agent count.
choices.rationale: You own reconciliation while monitoring. The runbook tells you what to push; you tell the runbook how it landed.

choices.label: Investigating a gap between installs and enrolments after 30 minutes.
choices.rationale: Tech work on the running rollout, not a runbook decision.

questions.explanation: Scope decisions are deployment-strategy items the runbook owns; the tech runs the documented push.

questions.prompt: What is the worst failure mode in bulk rollout per the lesson?

choices.label: A small percentage of endpoints fail to install on first push.
choices.rationale: Normal and manageable. Not the worst outcome.

choices: Enrolling agents into the wrong customer's organisation.

choices.label: Some endpoints take 30 minutes to enrol after the RMM job completes.
choices.rationale: Slow is normal; not a failure mode at all.

choices.label: The RMM job logs grow too large to parse quickly.
choices.rationale: Operational annoyance, not the worst outcome.

questions.explanation: Because the installer was bundled with the wrong org key. Bulk wrong-customer enrolment is why the global Agents view sanity check exists in the first ten minutes of a push.

questions.prompt: You push the rollout to a 147-workstation OU at 8:00pm. By 8:35pm the RMM shows 142 successful installs (5 known-offline). The portal shows 109 agents enrolled and is still climbing. What do you do?

choices.label: Re-push to the missing endpoints to speed things up.
choices.rationale: The trickle is normal and the gap will likely close in the next 20-30 minutes. Re-pushing creates duplicate-install attempts on machines mid-enrolment.

choices.label: Cancel the RMM job and start over to get a cleaner picture.
choices.rationale: Lose visibility into which endpoints already installed, create duplicate-push problems on the 109 that did enrol, and reset the clock for no reason.

questions.explanation: If the count is still moving it's slow, not stalled. Slow vs. stalled. As long as the count is moving, it's still working. Wait, then investigate if it stops.

questions.prompt: An hour later, the count is stuck at 138 agents for the last 15 minutes. The four missing endpoints are all in one site office. What do you do?

choices.label: Re-push to those four specifically.
choices.rationale: Reasonable instinct, premature execution. Re-pushing without understanding the pattern repeats the same failure.

choices: Investigate the pattern first, different AV, different egress firewall.

choices.label: Mark the four as failures and move on; they're a small percentage.
choices.rationale: Coverage gaps you don't investigate today become the 'missing endpoints' findings at coverage verification tomorrow.

questions.explanation: Or any compatibility difference the runbook didn't surface. Pattern-first, action-second. A cluster of failures usually has a single cause; the re-push is the right next step once you know what it is.

questions.prompt: Which pattern indicates the rollout has actually stalled (versus just slow)?

choices.label: Agents trickling in at an uneven rate, a batch of 20 in two minutes, then a pause, then another batch.
choices.rationale: That's slow. Uneven trickle is normal as endpoints phone home post-install at different times.

choices: No new agents in 30 or more minutes despite the RMM job claiming success; the gap has stopped closing.

choices.label: The portal shows fewer agents than the RMM after the first ten minutes.
choices.rationale: Expected. Agents take minutes to phone home post-install.

choices.label: The RMM job reports 100% success.
choices.rationale: That's the install side. Stalled is defined by portal-side movement, not RMM completion.

questions.explanation: Stalled is when the count stops moving. Now it's worth investigating, AV blocking, blocked outbound, or a wrong org key.

questions.prompt: Why does the runbook usually call for pushing the rollout out-of-hours, even though the install is silent?
choices: Silent installs aren't truly silent on every endpoint configuration.

choices.label: Huntress charges a premium for installs during business hours.
choices.rationale: Not a real commercial mechanic.

choices.label: Out-of-hours pushes complete faster because the network is less busy.
choices.rationale: Not the lesson's reason. The reason is user impact, not network throughput.

choices.label: Daytime pushes can trigger licensing checks that fail.
choices.rationale: No real licensing-by-hour mechanic.

questions.explanation: UAC prompts, AV alerts. Or noticeable performance blips can still hit users at their desks. User impact, not throughput. Silent on most endpoints doesn't mean silent on every endpoint.

questions.prompt: What's the single fastest sanity check that catches a wrong-customer enrolment in the first ten minutes of a rollout?

choices.label: Compare the RMM job log against your runbook checklist.
choices.rationale: Useful pre-flight, but it tells you what you pushed, not where it landed.

choices: Open the global Agents view, filter by enrolment time.

choices.label: Wait 24 hours and reconcile against AD.
choices.rationale: That's coverage verification's job. Too late to catch wrong-customer enrolment in the first 10 minutes.

choices.label: Open a Huntress support ticket asking which org the new agents are in.
choices.rationale: The portal exposes the answer directly.

questions.explanation: Confirm new agents all belong to the intended customer org. The global Agents view filtered by enrolment time is the named sanity check for the worst failure mode.

questions.prompt: Put the pre-flight checks in the order they should run before pushing the bulk install.
items: Confirm the right organisation key is bundled with the installer or referenced by the RMM job.
Confirm the target endpoint list matches the runbook's scope (workstation OU, server group, etc.).
Confirm the install window matches the runbook's intended time (usually out-of-hours).
Confirm AV/RMM compatibility steps from the runbook are done, exclusions, signed RMM script, log paths.
If anything above isn't right, stop and bump rather than push with an incomplete pre-flight.
questions.explanation: Right key first (the worst failure mode), then scope, then window, then compatibility, then stop-and-bump if anything is off. Don't improvise around a gap.

questions.prompt: You realise mid-push that 40 agents have landed in the wrong customer's organisation. What's the right move?

choices.label: Try to move them back to the right org yourself using the bulk agent tools.
choices.rationale: Agent relocation between orgs is a senior-owned bulk procedure, not a tech action.

choices: Stop the rollout. Bump to a senior with the specific state: which agents, which org, how many.

choices.label: Let the rollout complete, then uninstall the wrong-org agents and re-push with the right key.
choices.rationale: Compounds the mistake. You'd have history, incidents, and noise on a customer who shouldn't have been touched.

choices.label: Switch the org key on the installer mid-job so future endpoints land in the right place.
choices.rationale: Doesn't fix the agents already in the wrong place, and changes the rollout state mid-flight without senior sign-off.

questions.explanation: Don't keep pushing into the wrong place; don't try to fix it yourself. Bump with the specific state.

questions.prompt: If the portal enrolment count is below the RMM success count 30 minutes into the push, re-pushing immediately to close the gap is the right reflex.
questions.explanation: Re-pushing too early creates duplicate-install attempts and noise. Wait for the trickle to settle, then investigate the gap pattern if it persists.
